perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall protect all of the disclosing party's Proprietary Information as confidential information and, except with the prior written consent of the disclosing party or as otherwise specifically provided herein, shall. not disclose, copy or distribute such Proprietary Information to any other individual, corporation or entity for a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall endeavor to protect the disclosing party's Proprietary Information as confidential information and, except with the prior written consent of the disclosing party or as otherwise specifically provided herein, may not disclose, copy or distribute such Proprietary Information to other individuals or entities.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall protect' to 'shall endeavor to protect' and 'shall not disclose' to 'may not disclose' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The phrase 'endeavor to protect' lacks a defined standard of care, and 'may not disclose' implies a possibility of disclosure under certain circumstances. This creates ambiguity and makes enforcement difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act requires a reasonable effort standard to maintain the secrecy of the information. By changing the language to 'endeavor to protect' in the contract, it makes it ambiguous whether this rises to the level of "reasonable effort" under the statute. If that phrase is interpreted to be a lower standard than reasonable efforts, it violates the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 66-152 Article 24. Trade Secrets Protection Act. § 66-152. Definitions. As used in this Article, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Misappropriation" means acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied authority or consent, unless such trade secret was arrived at by independent development, reverse engineering, or was obtained from another person with a right to disclose the trade secret. (2) "Person" means an individual, corporation, government, governmental subdivision or agency, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, or any other legal or commercial entity. (3) "Trade secret" means business or technical information, including but not limited to a formula, pattern, program, device, compilation of information, method, technique, or process that: a. Derives independent actual or potential commercial value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable through independent development or reverse engineering by persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and b. Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. The existence of a trade secret shall not be negated merely because the information comprising the trade secret has also been developed, used, or owned independently by more than one person, or licensed to other persons. (1981, c. 890, s. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall protect' to 'shall endeavor to protect' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting the requirement for 'efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, G.S. 66-152 (3)(b). 'Endeavor to protect' introduces ambiguity and may not meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard, thus creating a contradiction. Additionally, changing 'shall not disclose' to 'may not disclose' implies a possibility of disclosure, further undermining the secrecy requirement.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall maintain the other party's Proprietary Information with at least the same degree of care each party uses to maintain its own proprietary information. Each party represents that such degree of care provides adequate protection for its own proprietary information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party should consider maintaining the other party's Proprietary Information with a degree of care similar to that used for its own proprietary information. Each party suggests that such consideration provides some protection for its own proprietary information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall maintain' with 'should consider maintaining' and 'provides adequate protection' with 'provides some protection' weakens the obligation to protect proprietary information, making the standard of care subjective and undefined. This ambiguity contradicts the requirement to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act outlines the protection of trade secrets. By weakening the language on each party to maintain a similar degree of care, it creates ambiguity around what is actually expected by each party, which is a potential violation of the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 66-152 Article 24. Trade Secrets Protection Act. § 66-152. Definitions. As used in this Article, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Misappropriation" means acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied authority or consent, unless such trade secret was arrived at by independent development, reverse engineering, or was obtained from another person with a right to disclose the trade secret. (2) "Person" means an individual, corporation, government, governmental subdivision or agency, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, or any other legal or commercial entity. (3) "Trade secret" means business or technical information, including but not limited to a formula, pattern, program, device, compilation of information, method, technique, or process that: a. Derives independent actual or potential commercial value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable through independent development or reverse engineering by persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and b. Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. The existence of a trade secret shall not be negated merely because the information comprising the trade secret has also been developed, used, or owned independently by more than one person, or licensed to other persons. (1981, c. 890, s. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text establishes a clear obligation for each party to maintain the other's proprietary information with a specific standard of care. The changed text weakens this obligation by using the language 'should consider maintaining' instead of 'shall maintain', and 'provides some protection' instead of 'provides adequate protection.' This introduces ambiguity about the required level of care. Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as the 'acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied authority or consent.' By weakening the obligation to protect trade secrets, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation due to the ambiguity surrounding the expected standard of care, therefore creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any documents or materials that are furnished by or on behalf of the disclosing party, and all other Proprietary Information in whatever form, including documents, reports, memoranda, notes, files or analyses prepared by or on behalf of the receiving party, including all copies of such materials, shall be promptly returned by the receiving party to the disclosing party upon written request by the disclosing party for any reason.
perturbation.changed_text: Documents or materials furnished by the disclosing party, and other Proprietary Information, may be returned by the receiving party to the disclosing party upon request for a valid reason.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be promptly returned' to 'may be returned' and removing 'all copies' weakens the obligation to return proprietary information. Adding 'for a valid reason' introduces subjective criteria for the return, giving the receiving party discretion. This undermines the protection of trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act demands that measures be put in place to make sure that trade secrets are protected. By altering the contract to allow the receiving party to return documentation only upon a 'valid reason,' it reduces the protection of trade secrets required to be maintained by the parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 66-152 Article 24. Trade Secrets Protection Act. § 66-152. Definitions. As used in this Article, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Misappropriation" means acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied authority or consent, unless such trade secret was arrived at by independent development, reverse engineering, or was obtained from another person with a right to disclose the trade secret. (2) "Person" means an individual, corporation, government, governmental subdivision or agency, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, or any other legal or commercial entity. (3) "Trade secret" means business or technical information, including but not limited to a formula, pattern, program, device, compilation of information, method, technique, or process that: a. Derives independent actual or potential commercial value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable through independent development or reverse engineering by persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and b. Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. The existence of a trade secret shall not be negated merely because the information comprising the trade secret has also been developed, used, or owned independently by more than one person, or licensed to other persons. (1981, c. 890, s. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially contradicting the North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act. The original contract mandated the prompt return of all proprietary information upon request. The modified version allows the receiving party discretion by stating that documents 'may be returned' only 'for a valid reason'. This introduces ambiguity and reduces the obligation to protect trade secrets. Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as 'acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied authority or consent'. By allowing the receiving party to potentially withhold proprietary information based on their own assessment of what constitutes a 'valid reason', the changed contract increases the risk of misappropriation as defined in the statute. The original language provides a clearer mechanism for protecting trade secrets by ensuring their prompt return. The modified language creates an ambiguity that could be exploited to delay or avoid the return of trade secrets, thereby undermining the protections intended by the Act.

perturbation.original_text: The construction, interpretation and performance of this Agreement, as well as the legal relations of the parties arising hereunder, will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of North Carolina.
perturbation.changed_text: The interpretation of this Agreement will be generally guided by legal principles with possible reference to the laws of North Carolina.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of North Carolina' with 'will be generally guided by legal principles with possible reference to the laws of North Carolina' introduces ambiguity as to which laws will govern the agreement. 'Generally guided' and 'possible reference' are vague and non-committal. This creates uncertainty and undermines the predictability of the agreement, which is important for enforcement and dispute resolution. It is a particular problem because it relates to trade secrets and the protection thereof.
perturbation.contradicted_law: North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act requires reasonable measures. Changing the contract to make it so that the agreement is only 'generally guided by legal principles with possible reference to the laws of North Carolina' reduces the protective measures required by the parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ G.S. 66-152 Article 24. Trade Secrets Protection Act. § 66-152. Definitions. As used in this Article, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Misappropriation" means acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied authority or consent, unless such trade secret was arrived at by independent development, reverse engineering, or was obtained from another person with a right to disclose the trade secret. (2) "Person" means an individual, corporation, government, governmental subdivision or agency, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, or any other legal or commercial entity. (3) "Trade secret" means business or technical information, including but not limited to a formula, pattern, program, device, compilation of information, method, technique, or process that: a. Derives independent actual or potential commercial value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable through independent development or reverse engineering by persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and b. Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. The existence of a trade secret shall not be negated merely because the information comprising the trade secret has also been developed, used, or owned independently by more than one person, or licensed to other persons. (1981, c. 890, s. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates that the agreement 'will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of North Carolina.' The changed text weakens this by stating the agreement 'will be generally guided by legal principles with possible reference to the laws of North Carolina.' This introduces ambiguity regarding which specific laws govern the agreement, especially concerning trade secrets. The North Carolina Trade Secrets Protection Act, as defined in G.S. 66-152, requires reasonable measures to maintain secrecy. By making the governing law less definitive, the changed text potentially reduces the 'reasonable' protective measures that the parties are obligated to take, creating a contradiction. The change moves from a definitive legal obligation to a suggestion and this undermines the protection that the law requires.
